content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob play the following game.They choose a number N to play with.The runs are as follows :



1.Bob plays first and the two players alternate.



2.In his/her turn ,a player can subtract from N any prime     number(including 1) less than N.The number thus obtained is the new N.



3.The person who cannot make a move in his/her turn loses the game.



Assuming both play optimally,who wins the game ?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing "ALICE" if the second player (Alice) wins with optimal play, otherwise output "BOB".

sampleTestCases.explanation: N=1 → 1 mod 4 == 1, so Alice wins. N=2 → 2 mod 4 != 1, so Bob wins.
5 and 9 are both 1 mod 4 → Alice wins. 10 mod 4 = 2 → Bob wins.
Only N=13 is 1 mod 4, so Alice wins; all others Bob wins.

solution.approach: The game is impartial and the set of moves from a position N is the set of numbers N - p where p is any prime (including 1) smaller than N. By analyzing the first few positions we observe a periodic pattern: N = 1 is a losing position (no move). N = 2,3,4 are winning because they can move to 1. N = 5 can only move to 4,3,2 – all winning – therefore 5 is losing. Continuing this reasoning shows that exactly the numbers congruent to 1 modulo 4 are losing positions, all others are winning. Hence Bob (the first player) loses iff N % 4 == 1, otherwise he wins. The optimal strategy is simply to check N modulo 4 for each test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
